title: FastAPI
summary: Python web framework for building modern APIs — async-first, built on Starlette + Pydantic.

children.text: Python web framework for building modern APIs
Built on Starlette (ASGI) + Uvicorn, with Pydantic for data
Speed & performance
Asynchronous support with async/await syntax
Built on top of Starlette and Pydantic
Automatic data validation and serialization
Automatic interactive API documentation
Dependency Injection system for easy management of components and logic
Security utilities — built-in support for authentication and authorization
Good developer experience — minimal boilerplate, clear errors, easy to test
WebSockets & streaming support
async, await & event loop
Concurrency without threads — single thread, event-loop based
async def creates a coroutine; await suspends it so the loop runs other tasks while waiting for I/O
FastAPI is built on Starlette + Uvicorn (ASGI) — same idea as Express + Node's libuv
If a function does I/O, make it async def and await it. If it's CPU-heavy (image processing, crypto), keep it plain def — FastAPI pushes it to a thread pool automatically.
Event Loop
Python's asyncio event loop is single-threaded and cooperative — same model as Node
Maintains a queue of coroutines; runs one until it hits an await on something not ready, then parks it and runs the next
When the awaited operation completes, the coroutine re-enters the queue to resume
A coroutine must voluntarily yield via await. A single time.sleep(2) inside an async route freezes every request on that worker for 2 full seconds.
Thread pool
Plain def routes don't run on the event loop — Starlette dispatches them via anyio.to_thread.run_sync() to a worker thread (default cap 40)
The event loop kicks off the dispatch (near-instant) and goes back to other requests; the blocking happens in a thread, not on the loop
Python's GIL (Global Interpreter Lock)
Only one thread executes Python bytecode at a time, even with multiple threads
So the thread pool gives parallel waiting, not parallel computation
Most blocking calls (network/file I/O, time.sleep) release the GIL while waiting, so other threads progress
For genuinely CPU-heavy work, reach for multiprocessing, a separate worker process, or an offline job (Celery/ARQ)
Practical decision tree
I/O + an async-native library exists → async def + await (asyncpg, httpx.AsyncClient, aioredis)
I/O but only a sync library → keep the route plain def; let FastAPI offload to the thread pool (don't call the sync lib from async def)
Pure CPU-bound → plain def is usually fine; if heavy and frequent, use a separate worker process
One Uvicorn worker = one event loop + one thread pool; run multiple workers (Gunicorn/Uvicorn --workers N) to scale across cores
Lifespan events
Initialize and clean up app-level resources (DB pool, ML model, Redis) once on startup and once on shutdown

children.code:
@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    # --- Startup ---
    app.state.db_pool = await create_pool(DATABASE_URL)
    app.state.redis = await aioredis.create_redis_pool(REDIS_URL)
    yield  # App runs here
    # --- Shutdown ---
    await app.state.db_pool.close()
    await app.state.redis.close()

app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)

children.text: Store shared resources on app.state; access via request.app.state.db_pool or inject as a dependency
async vs normal functions
By default routes run in separate threads; an async function is handled in the same thread and won't block other requests while waiting on I/O
Async needs supported drivers: asyncpg (PostgreSQL), aiomysql (MySQL); sessionmaker/create_engine also change for async
App init changes too — lifespan creates tables and disposes the engine on shutdown
Relationship queries need eager loading (e.g. selectinload) to avoid multiple queries
Async SQLAlchemy + asyncpg
Regular SQLAlchemy blocks the event loop — db.query() is synchronous
Use SQLAlchemy 2.0 async mode with the asyncpg driver for non-blocking queries
N+1 trap: lazy loading doesn't work in async mode. Use selectinload() or joinedload() to eagerly load relationships in one query.
FastAPI() — initialize the app
Body() — returns data sent through the body; only for POST, PUT, PATCH
GET request
Decorator for the GET method on a path; add a success status code if you want
Order matters — define static paths before dynamic ones.
POST request
Create data; body data is passed to the argument
Path parameters (dynamic paths)
@app.get("/product/{id}") — id is passed to the function; variable names must match
Query parameters
Same key name must be a function argument, e.g. /result/{id}/?page=1 → func(id, page)
Middleware
Wraps every request — CORS, logging, rate limiting, request IDs, timing

children.code:
@app.middleware("http")
async def add_timing_header(request: Request, call_next):
    start = time.time()
    response = await call_next(request)   # call_next = next()
    response.headers["X-Process-Time"] = str(time.time() - start)
    return response

children.text: Exception handling
@app.exception_handler(ExceptionType) — handle exceptions globally, e.g. RequestValidationError
HTTPException
Background Tasks
Fire-and-forget work after the response is sent — emails, analytics, webhooks; runs in the same process

children.code:
from fastapi import BackgroundTasks

@app.post("/users")
async def create_user(user: UserCreate, background_tasks: BackgroundTasks):
    new_user = await db.create(user)
    # Scheduled — doesn't block the response
    background_tasks.add_task(send_welcome_email, new_user.email)
    return new_user  # returns immediately

children.text: Don't use BackgroundTasks for work over a few seconds, anything needing retries, or tasks that must survive a crash — use Celery + Redis.
Celery — more complex background task management and scheduling
APIRouter
Split routes into files; use router instead of app in route files, then app.include_router(auth_router)
StaticFiles
Jinja2Templates
Render HTML templates: templates.TemplateResponse("home.html", {"request": request, "title": "Home"})
if/else, for loops, block (override in child templates), extends (inherit a base), url_for (build URLs for static/routes)
Starlette
ASGI framework FastAPI is built on — routing, middleware, background tasks
Non-existing routes raise StarletteHTTPException, which you can catch and handle
Pydantic
Data modeling, parsing and error handling; type validation at runtime with clear messages
BaseModel — data model classes extend it
response_model — specify a route's response model
Field — validation rules and metadata for fields
ConfigDict — configure model behavior; from_attributes populates fields from object attributes (useful with ORMs)
Validation helpers
Path — path param validation
Query — query param validation
Type hints & Annotated
Type hints are metadata on variables/params; Python doesn't enforce them at runtime
Annotated = type + extra metadata; FastAPI reads it to know e.g. 'this is a query param' or 'validate >= 0'
Depends()
Declare a function as a dependency; FastAPI calls it before your route and passes the result in
Used to share DB sessions, current user, config, rate limiters, caches
FastAPI builds a dependency graph and resolves everything before the route; two routes that Depends(get_db) each get their own session per request
Engine, session & base
create_engine — create a SQLAlchemy engine for the DB connection
sessionmaker — session factory for managing DB sessions
DeclarativeBase — base class for models
get_db — create a session and yield it for use in route handlers
db dependency injection — inject a session into a handler
children.note: def create_user(user: UserCreate, db: Annotated[Session, Depends(get_db)])

children.text: Models & Base

children.code:
class Base(DeclarativeBase):
    pass

class User(Base):
    __tablename__ = "users"
    id: Mapped[int]          = mapped_column(primary_key=True)
    email: Mapped[str]       = mapped_column(String(255), unique=True, index=True)
    name: Mapped[str | None] = mapped_column(String(100))
    posts: Mapped[list["Post"]] = relationship(back_populates="author", lazy="select")

children.text: Relationships
One-to-many: cascade="all, delete-orphan" — deleting a user deletes all their posts
One-to-one: relationship(..., uselist=False)

children.code:
# Many-to-many via an association table
tags: Mapped[list["Tag"]] = relationship(
    "Tag", secondary=post_tags, back_populates="posts"
)

children.text: Lazy loading strategies
lazy="select" (default) — separate SELECT on first access; easy but causes N+1
lazy="joined" — LEFT OUTER JOIN in one query; best for small related sets always loaded
lazy="subquery" — one extra subquery per collection; good for large collections without cartesian explosion
lazy="raise" — raises on access; enforces explicit eager loading, great for prod safety
N+1 is the #1 SQLAlchemy performance killer. Use lazy="raise" in production and explicit selectinload()/joinedload() per query.
Eager loading
selectinload — 2 queries, no data duplication; use for collections
joinedload — 1 query with JOIN; use for to-one (author, profile)
Engine setup

children.code:
engine = create_engine(
    "postgresql+psycopg2://user:pass@localhost/mydb",
    pool_size=10,          # persistent connections
    max_overflow=20,       # extra connections allowed
    pool_pre_ping=True,    # validate connection before use (avoids stale conns)
    pool_recycle=3600,     # recycle connections every hour
    echo=False,            # True logs all SQL — dev only
)
SessionLocal = sessionmaker(
    bind=engine, autoflush=False, autocommit=False, expire_on_commit=False
)

children.text: expire_on_commit=False keeps attribute values accessible after commit without re-querying — important in async/API contexts
Password hashing — pwdlib; recommended() returns the most secure algorithm available
JWT (JSON Web Tokens) — access tokens for authentication
OAuth2 framework — Swagger auto-generates the login UI; OAuth2PasswordBearer extracts and validates the token
Manage schema changes over time, like git for your DB

children.code:
alembic revision --autogenerate -m "Add new column to users table"
alembic upgrade head      # apply pending migrations
alembic downgrade -1      # roll back the most recent migration
alembic history          # show migration history

children.text: Execute multiple Redis commands atomically on the server — avoids race conditions and round trips

children.code:
local balance = tonumber(redis.call("GET", KEYS[1]))
if balance >= tonumber(ARGV[1]) then
    redis.call("DECRBY", KEYS[1], ARGV[1])
    return 1
end
return 0

children.text: Redis guarantees no other client runs commands in the middle — the script completes before anything else executes
